visits.text:
First stop in Busan: Gamcheon Culture Village, nicknamed the 'Machu Picchu of Busan'. This wasn't an artists' village—it was a 1950s shantytown built on a hillside by war refugees.

The colorful houses are the result of an artistic renovation project launched in 2009. The maze-like alleyways preserve their residential character—families still live here.

Head down to Jagalchi Market, Korea's largest seafood market. The ground floor has auctioned catches since dawn.

Head upstairs—they'll cook on the spot what you just bought downstairs.

Gwangan Bridge seen from Gwangalli Beach in the evening—a 4.6-mile suspension bridge that gradually lights up after sunset.

The waterfront is lined with open-air cafes and bars frequented by locals.

BIFF Square in Nampo-dong district—the plaza of the Busan International Film Festival, where Korean cinema stars' handprints are engraved in the ground.

Busan's IFEZ attracts Asia's greatest directors every October.

Begin with Haedong Yonggungsa Temple, founded in 1376—one of Korea's rare temples built directly on the seashore. The 108 steps (the number of human desires in Buddhism) descend toward the sea alongside stone lanterns.

Destroyed in 1592, renamed 'Temple of the Sea Dragon Palace' in 1974 after a vision by monk Jeong-am. The seven-story pagoda guards the entrance facing the waves.

Reach Haeundae Beach—1.1 miles of sand, Korea's most famous. In summer, 1.5 million people pack in during August. In winter, the Winter Swimming Club has taken the dawn plunge here since 1964.

Dongbaek Island at the beach's eastern tip is connected by a breakwater and offers views across the entire bay.

Taejongdae Park on Busan's southern tip—65-foot cliffs plunging into the sea, pine forest, 19th-century lighthouse.

On clear days, you can spot the Japanese island of Tsushima 31 miles away.

Dongbaek Island at the eastern end of Haeundae, connected by a breakwater—panoramic view across the entire Haeundae Bay and the skyscraper district behind.

The trail circles the island in 30 minutes.

Head north of Busan to Beomeosa Temple, founded in 678, on the slope of Mount Geumjeong. Less touristy than Haedong, more authentic.

Its name means 'temple of the sacred carp from heaven'—according to legend, a golden carp would descend to play in the fountain from the clouds. The four-pillar main gate, dating from 1614, is a National Treasure.

Continue to Geumjeongsanseong Fortress on the ridge at 2,625 feet—10.5 miles of ramparts, four gates, 360° view over Busan and the sea. An overlooked fortress.

Ascent by cable car from Beomeosa, descent on foot along the ramparts.

Busan Museum in Nam-gu district—collections tracing the city's history from prehistory through the Korean War.

Free admission, rarely visited by foreign tourists.

Busan's North Port area in late afternoon—the old port warehouses renovated into galleries, restaurants, and terraces overlooking the bay and container ships.

The port rehabilitation draws direct inspiration from Hamburg and Rotterdam models.

activities.text: Dinner in Nampo-dong district—octopus pancakes, fresh oysters at Gukje Market, pig's feet soup. Busan is Korea's unofficial gastronomic capital.
Sunset on Gwangalli Beach with a view of the illuminated Gwangan Bridge—locals' favorite spot, 20 minutes by subway from Nampo.
Karaoke in Seomyeon district after dinner—the norebang (private booth by the hour) is a Korean institution, open until dawn.
Evening stroll along the Oncheon River quays in Dongrae district—less touristy than Haeundae, lively residential neighborhood atmosphere.
Walk along the Haeundae Beach boardwalk—1.1 miles of promenade lined with cafes and seafood restaurants. Lively even off-season.
Haejangguk (Busan beef soup) dinner at a Jagalchi Market restaurant—the quintessential local dish, served from dawn for fishermen and night owls.
Visit Haeundae night market—grilled seafood, tteokbokki, pajeon (scallion pancake). Lively in the evening, right on the beach.
Evening walk on the Dongbaek Island breakwater—view of Haeundae lights from the sea, quiet and uncrowded after 9 PM.
Hike along the ramparts from the north gate—1.2 miles with alternating views over the city and wooded hills. About 2 hours.
Sunset on Gwangalli Beach with a view of Gwangan Bridge—4.6 miles of suspension bridge illuminated at night. Locals' favorite spot in Busan.
Last evening in Busan in Seomyeon district—shopping mall, restaurants, bars. The city's liveliest district outside Haeundae.
Last drink in a Busan makgeolli bar—local rice wine served cold with pajeon (pancakes). Bars in Seomyeon district serve until dawn.

days_plan.itin_title: South Korea Grand Tour: Seoul, Gyeongju & Busan
South Korea Grand Tour: Seoul, Gyeongju & Busan
South Korea Grand Tour: Seoul, Gyeongju & Busan
Complete South Korea: Seoul, Busan & Jeju
Complete South Korea: Seoul, Busan & Jeju
Complete South Korea: Seoul, Busan & Jeju

visits.text:
Welcome to Gyeongju—former capital of the Silla kingdom for a thousand years, inscribed entirely on UNESCO as a 'museum without walls'.

The Daereungwon site brings together 23 royal tumuli right in the city center, some reaching 72 feet in height. Cheonmachong tomb, excavated in 1973, yielded a gold crown, 11,500 objects, and the only known Silla painting—a white winged horse.

Continue to Cheomseongdae Observatory, built around 632-647 under Queen Seondeok—the oldest astronomical observatory still standing in East Asia.

The 30-foot tower is assembled from 362 granite stones, the number of days in the lunar calendar of the era. A woman ruler in the 7th century: exceptional for East Asia.

End at Anapji Pond at sunset—royal Silla palace garden built in 674. In 1975, drainage work brought up 33,000 artifacts from the water's bottom: royal tableware, game pieces, boat models.

The pond is illuminated at night and reflects in the water—one of Korea's most photographed panoramas.

Woljeonggyo Bridge, reconstructed in 2018 to match the 8th-century royal Silla bridge. Illuminated at night, it spans the Namcheon River in the heart of Gyeongju.

One of the city's most photographed night views.

Head toward Mount Tohamsan to Bulguksa, built in 528 and reconstructed in 751 by Prime Minister Kim Daeseong—according to legend, to appease his parents' souls.

The temple houses six National Treasures, including the twin pagodas Dabotap and Seokgatap facing each other in the central courtyard for 1,250 years. UNESCO-listed in 1995.

Climb up to Seokguram, 2.5 miles from Bulguksa and 2,460 feet elevation. This artificial grotto carved from granite—Korean granite doesn't lend itself to direct carving—was built for Kim Daeseong in memory of his parents from a previous life.

Its 22-foot dome shelters an 11-foot Buddha gazing toward the East Sea. The grotto was rediscovered by chance in 1909 by a man seeking shelter from the rain.

Yangdong Village, UNESCO-listed in 2010—one of Korea's best-preserved hanok villages, founded in the 15th century by two aristocratic Joseon clans.

About sixty traditional houses still inhabited, nestled in a valley 30 minutes from Gyeongju.

Mount Namsan of Gyeongju, sacred mountain of the Silla kingdom covered with rock-carved Buddhas, pagodas, and temple ruins—over 150 Buddhist sites scattered across its slopes.

The shortest hike (Sambulsa → Yongjangsaji) is 2.5 miles and passes the three most famous Buddhas.

activities.text: Rent a bike or e-bike near the station to explore the historic center—tumuli, steles, and pagodas are scattered through the city like in a park. Flat terrain, the loop is about 9 miles.
Ssambap tasting (rice wrapped in vegetable leaves) in a traditional Gyeongju restaurant—local cuisine is renowned for recipes passed down since the Silla era.
Evening walk around illuminated Anapji Pond—the reflections of the pavilions in the water are Gyeongju's most photographed panorama.
Night walk through the streets of downtown Gyeongju—the lighting of tumuli and historic sites creates a unique atmosphere after nightfall. Start with the illuminated Woljeonggyo Bridge.
Between Bulguksa and Seokguram, hike up the forest trail rather than taking the bus—2.5 miles through a pine forest (1.5 hours). The descent can be done by bus.
Visit Gyeongju Traditional Market (Jungang Market)—local vegetables, Korean snacks, artisanal tofu. Open daily, liveliest in the morning.
Gyeongju bread tasting (hwangnam ppang)—small pastry filled with red bean paste, the city's specialty since the 1920s. Bakeries are downtown.

Forest bathing on the slopes of Mount Tohamsan after descending from Seokguram—the centuries-old pines on the trail are classified as a therapeutic resource by the Korean government. Free, accessible from Bulguksa parking lot.

days_plan.itin_title: South Korea Grand Tour: Seoul, Gyeongju & Busan
South Korea Grand Tour: Seoul, Gyeongju & Busan
Complete South Korea: Seoul, Busan & Jeju
Complete South Korea: Seoul, Busan & Jeju

visits.text:
Start by climbing Seongsan Ilchulbong first thing in the morning—this volcanic cone burst from the sea 5,000 years ago, and it shows.

Twenty minutes of ascent brings you to a bowl-shaped crater open to the sky, ringed by vertical cliffs plunging into the sea. UNESCO-listed.

Continue to Manjanggul Cave, a lava tube carved 100,000 to 300,000 years ago.

One kilometer of gallery with frozen lava ceilings and, at the back, a 25-foot stalagmite—the world's tallest in a lava tube. It's a constant 52°F, bring a jacket.

Jeju Stone Park—open-air museum tracing the island's geological and cultural formation through basalt sculptures and gardens.

The dol hareubang (big stone grandfathers) are Jeju's symbol, village protectors since the 18th century.

Seongsan village—small fishing port at the foot of Ilchulbong crater, still inhabited by traditional fishing families.

The morning market starts before dawn, with haenyeo returning from their dives.

Hallasan, 6,398 feet—the roof of South Korea. Shield volcano with 360 parasitic cones all around, the summit crater holds a lake that disappears under snow much of the year.

The ascent takes 3 to 5 hours depending on the trail chosen. Advance online reservation required before departure.

Jusangjeolli Cliffs on the south coast—65- to 82-foot hexagonal basalt columns rising from the sea. A half-mile trail runs along the cliffs, the spectacle is better at low tide.

Formed by rapid cooling of lava in seawater.

Jungmun Saekdal Beach, the south coast's most beautiful beach—black volcanic sand mixed with white sand, powerful waves favored by surfers.

The Teddy Bear Museum and Jeju Loveland are nearby if you want a lighthearted break.

Jeju Haenyeo Research Center in Hado—exhibition on the history and diving techniques of the female divers, UNESCO-listed in 2016.

Demonstrations available depending on schedule.

Head down to the south coast to Jusangjeolli—65- to 82-foot hexagonal basalt columns rising from the sea like a giant pipe organ.

It's the result of rapid cooling of lava in seawater. A half-mile trail runs along the cliffs, the spectacle is better at low tide.

Then climb to Sanbangsan, a 1,296-foot lava dome sitting alone on the west coast.

Midway up, a cave houses a Buddha statue and water constantly seeps from the wall—legend says these are the tears of the mountain deity.

Finish the day at Cheonjeyeon Falls—72-foot cascade at the bottom of a black basalt canyon.

To reach it, cross Seonim Bridge adorned with seven stone nymphs, which spans the canyon just above the third waterfall.

activities.text:
In late afternoon, set out on Olle Trail #1—a 9-mile coastal path along the sea with Seongsan Ilchulbong as backdrop.

Walk as far as you like, a partial round-trip from Sinyangri works perfectly.
Sample black pork (흑돼지) at dinner—Jeju black pork is the island's main specialty, grilled over charcoal in restaurants in Jeju City's Heuk-dwaeji district.
Visit Seongsan village at sunset—the alleys of the small fishing port at the foot of Ilchulbong crater are quiet after 6 PM, when tour buses have left.
Watch haenyeo at Seongsan port early morning—the female divers return with their catch before 8 AM.
In the evening, check out Dongmun Market in Jeju City—fresh tangerines, sweet potato pancakes, black carrot juice. The island's liveliest market in the evening.
Hallasan hike—ascend via Eorimok trail (3 miles, 2,300-foot elevation gain) to Witseoreum plateau, or Seongpanak trail (5.6 miles) to the summit. Online reservation required.
Stroll through Jeju City's Chilseong-ro district—lively shopping street with local boutiques, cafés, and black pork restaurants.
Sample tangerine makgeolli at a bar in Jeju City—rice alcohol with Jeju tangerines, a specialty unavailable elsewhere. Downtown bars serve it with sweet potato pancakes.

Early morning, go see the haenyeo at Sehwa or Hado—these women dive without tanks in apnea to 30–65 feet to gather sea urchins, abalone, and octopus.

Their practice has been UNESCO-listed since 2016. Arrive before 10 AM, that's when they're in action.
Stroll on Jungmun Beach or in Seogwipo—Jeju's second city, calmer and greener than Jeju City, with its seaside gardens.
Dinner of galbi-tang (beef rib soup) in Seogwipo—specialty of southern Jeju, simmered for hours. Restaurants around Seogwipo Market are the best.

Evening walk in Seogwipo Park along the cliffs—Jeongbang Falls (75 feet directly into the sea) are illuminated in the evening. One of the few waterfalls in the world that falls directly into the ocean.

days_plan.itin_title: Complete South Korea: Seoul, Busan & Jeju
Complete South Korea: Seoul, Busan & Jeju
Complete South Korea: Seoul, Busan & Jeju

visits.text:
Arrival in Jeonju by KTX. The hanok village gathers 800 traditional houses in the historic quarter—Jeonju was the spiritual capital of the Joseon Dynasty, birthplace of founding king Taejo.

Gyeonggijeon, built in 1410, houses his official portrait. Jeondong Cathedral (1914) stands on the site where Korea's first Catholic martyrs were executed in the 19th century.

Climb to Omokdae Pavilion on the hill north of the village—this is where Yi Seong-gye celebrated his victory over Japanese invaders in 1380, 12 years before founding the Joseon Dynasty.

Panoramic view over the hanok village below.

Jeondong Cathedral (1914), in Romano-Byzantine style, built on the site of the first executions of Korean Catholics in the 19th century.

Same architect as Seoul's Myeongdong Cathedral.

The Jeonju National Museum—collections of Baekje Buddhist art and Joseon objects. Free admission.

Good complement to the village for understanding the history of the Jeolla region.

activities.text:
Bibimbap lunch in the village—Jeonju is classified as a UNESCO Creative City of Gastronomy and birthplace of bibimbap. The rice is cooked in beef broth, not water.

Try the yukhoe bibimbap version with beef tartare—it's the local specialty.
Rent a hanbok in the village for an evening stroll through the cobbled alleyways.
Makgeolli (rice wine) tasting in a traditional bar in the hanok village—Jeonju is also renowned for its makgeolli bars that serve free accompaniments (anju) with each order.
Evening walk through the cobbled alleyways of the hanok village—the dim lantern lighting gives the quarter another face. Fewer crowds after 8 PM.

days_plan.itin_title: South Korea Grand Tour: Seoul, Gyeongju & Busan
Complete South Korea: Seoul, Busan & Jeju

visits.text:
Start at Gyeongbokgung right when it opens—the largest palace of the Joseon Dynasty, covering 100 acres, built in 1395.

It was burned by the Japanese in 1592, rebuilt in 1867, then demolished by 80% between 1910 and 1945. Restoration continues to this day. Arrive for the changing of the guard at 11 AM. If you're wearing a hanbok, admission is free.

Head north to Bukchon—900 traditional wooden houses from the 1920s-1940s, wedged between two palaces.

During Japanese colonization, a Korean developer deliberately built these hanok to prevent Japanese buyers from acquiring the land. The alleys of Gahoe-dong offer the classic view of curved rooftops with N Seoul Tower in the background. Access restricted 10 AM-5 PM since November 2024—speak quietly, people actually live here.

Finish at Changdeokgung, the best-preserved of Seoul's five palaces, a UNESCO World Heritage site. Built in 1405, it replaced Gyeongbokgung for 250 years after its destruction.

The Secret Garden to the north—80 acres of pavilions, ponds, and forests—can only be visited by reservation in guided groups. Book ahead.

The National Palace Museum of Korea, right at the entrance to Gyeongbokgung—collections of royal furniture, court costumes, everyday objects from life inside the Joseon palaces.

Free admission, less crowded than the palace itself. A good starting point before your visit.

Begin at Jongmyo Shrine, the quietest and most awe-inspiring place in Seoul. Built in 1395, it houses the funeral tablets of 19 Joseon kings and 30 queens.

The main hall stretches 330 feet long—Korea's longest straight wooden building. Every first Sunday in May, the royal Jongmyo Jerye ceremony unfolds here with ancestral costumes, music, and dances, inscribed on UNESCO's list.

Head to Gwangjang Market for lunch—opened in 1905, Seoul's oldest covered market still operating.

Order the yukhoe (beef tartare), bindaetteok (fried mung bean pancake), or spicy mini rice rolls. Halmeoni in aprons have been cooking here since dawn.

In the evening, climb N Seoul Tower on Namsan Mountain—1,571 feet elevation, 360° views over illuminated Seoul.

The Namsan fortress walls snake around the hill with sections dating to the 17th century. Take the cable car or hike up the trail (30 minutes).

The Dongdaemun Design Plaza (DDP) by Zaha Hadid—over 900,000 square feet without a single right angle, open 24/7. During construction, archaeologists unearthed 1,600 artifacts from a 14th-century fortress.

The Heunginjimun Gate, a remnant of Seoul's old ramparts, stands right next door.

Head to Gangnam to see the Starfield Library in the COEX mall—a 43-foot-high hall entirely lined with books, now one of Seoul's most photographed spots.

A 10-minute walk away, Bongeunsa Temple (794 AD) offers a striking contrast: 37,000 square feet of Buddhist gardens at the foot of Gangnam's skyscrapers.

Then head up to the Seoul Sky observatory at Lotte World Tower, on the 123rd floor—1,821 feet high, the world's 5th tallest tower.

The glass floor at 1,640 feet is reserved for thrill-seekers. Views stretch across the entire city, the Han River, and the mountains beyond.

The National Museum of Korea, Asia's largest at 3.5 million square feet. Free admission, 12,000 years of Korean history.

Don't miss the 7th-century Baekje gilt-bronze incense burner—one of the finest pieces of Korean art.

The Apgujeong-Rodeo neighborhood in Gangnam—Korean designer boutiques, luxury cafés, contemporary art galleries. Seoul's most upscale district, the opposite of the historic palace areas.

This is where K-beauty and Korean cosmetic surgery have their most visible showcase.

Seoul Olympic Park, built for the 1988 Olympics in the Songpa district. Five sports venues surrounded by contemporary sculptures and a velodrome.

The site also preserves remnants of a Baekje kingdom fortress (4th century) unearthed during pre-Olympic excavations.

The Third Infiltration Tunnel, discovered in 1978, 240 feet beneath the border. Dug in secret, it runs over a mile long and could have moved 30,000 soldiers per hour toward the South.

The walls were painted black by North Korea to make it look like a coal mine—there's no coal in this region.

Dora Observatory, the most advanced observation point facing north. On clear days, you can spot Kaesong and Kijong-dong, the North Korean 'propaganda village'—empty buildings with programmed lights.

Binoculars are provided on-site. Photography is prohibited in certain zones.

The Bridge of Freedom, across which 12,773 prisoners of war crossed south in 1953. The barbed wire lining it is covered with colored ribbons tied by families separated by the border for 70 years.

Imjingak Park around the bridge preserves a Korean War tank and a bullet-riddled locomotive found on the Gyeongui railway line.

Dorasan Station, the last South Korean station before the border—built in 2002 in hopes of reunification, it has never hosted regular trains to the North.

The departure board still displays 'Pyongyang' as a destination. The waiting room sits empty.

Start at Deoksugung Palace, the last built during the Joseon era, right in the heart of the modern city center.

Emperor Gojong lived here after Queen Min's assassination in 1895. The changing of the guard in medieval costumes happens three times daily at Daehanmun Gate. The street along its walls is called 'Breakup Street'—a local tradition says couples who walk here will split up.

In the afternoon, climb the Seoul City Wall around Naksan Mountain—restored sections of the 1396 wall that encircled the Joseon capital for 11 miles.

Nighttime views over the old quarters from the rampart walk. Plan for 90 minutes of walking.

The Seochon neighborhood and its hanok—artist studios, hidden galleries, tea houses in century-old buildings west of Gyeongbokgung. Poet Yi Sang lived here.

More authentic than Insadong, fewer tourists.

Cheongdam Bridge and the Cheongdam-dong neighborhood—contemporary art galleries, Korean concept stores, and design cafés in the alleys perpendicular to Apgujeong.

Less famous than Gangnam but more interesting for current Korean art and design.

Spend the morning at the National Museum of Korea—3.5 million square feet, 12,000 years of Korean history, free admission.

Don't miss the 7th-century Baekje gilt-bronze incense burner, the crown jewel of the collections. Allow at least 2 hours.

Stop by Namdaemun Market before you leave—open since 1414, one of Asia's oldest covered markets still operating.

Ginseng, artisan noodles, clothing. Sungnyemun Gate stands at the entrance—Seoul's oldest wooden structure, built in 1398, burned down in 2008 and restored by 2013.

Gyeonghuigung Palace, the least known of Seoul's five palaces, now partially restored in a quiet residential neighborhood west of Gyeongbokgung.

Built in 1617, it served as a secondary residence for Joseon kings. Much less crowded than the other palaces.

Han River Park for a final moment by the river—settle on the banks, order ramyeon through a Korean app (waterside delivery is a local tradition).

Bikes for rent on-site to ride along the banks to Banpo Bridge.

activities.text: Rent a hanbok near Anguk Station—free admission to all of Seoul's palaces and better photos in the Bukchon alleys.
Lunch in the Bukchon alleys—the sundubu jjigae restaurants (silky tofu in spicy broth) around Anguk are among Seoul's best.
Evening stroll along the Cheonggyecheon—an urban stream reopened in 2005, running 3.6 miles, illuminated and lively at night. A classic Seoul locals' walk.
Evening in the Jongno-3ga neighborhood—its pojangmacha (heated street tents) welcome night owls over soju and meat skewers or pajeon. The vibe after 9 PM is quintessentially Seoul.

After dinner, take a walk along the Cheonggyecheon—an urban stream reopened in 2005 after 40 years beneath a highway.

3.6 miles of illuminated banks, lively in the evening. A classic Seoul locals' stroll.
In the afternoon, wander through Insadong—antique dealers, calligraphy galleries, tea houses, craft shops. On Sundays, the street is closed to cars.
Korean barbecue in the evening in the Mapo neighborhood—samgyeopsal or galbi grilled over charcoal at your table, lettuce wraps, banchan.
Evening in Hongdae—bars, clubs, food trucks, street musicians. Seoul's liveliest student quarter after midnight.
Korean cooking class in Insadong or Hapjeong—kimchi, bibimbap, or tteokbokki depending on the program. About 3 hours, advance booking required.
Visit the MMCA Seoul (National Museum of Modern and Contemporary Art) in the former military barracks near Gyeongbokgung. Paid admission, Korean and international collections.
Korean barbecue in the evening in the Yongsan neighborhood—galbi and naengmyeon in restaurants around the station. Local neighborhood vibe, off the tourist trail.
Evening in the Sinchon neighborhood—student bars, pojangmacha, and grilled fish restaurants. More local and cheaper than Hongdae, just two subway stops away.
Organized tour from Seoul—passport required, minimum 48 hours advance booking. Total duration about 7 hours. JSA/Panmunjom tours have been suspended since 2023.
Evening stroll on Banpo Bridge and the Rainbow Fountain—9,500 nozzles projecting illuminated colored water over the Han. Shows every hour in the evening.
Evening in Itaewon upon returning from the DMZ—restaurants from around the world, lively bars. Seoul's international neighborhood, 10 minutes from downtown by subway.
Dinner in Yongsan on your way back—a stone's throw from the War Memorial, Korean restaurants serving galbi and naengmyeon (cold noodles), local neighborhood atmosphere.
End the evening in Mangwon or Mapo—local market, grilled fish restaurants, residential neighborhood vibe off the tourist circuit.
Temple stay overnight at Bongeunsa Temple in Gangnam—4:30 AM wake-up, meditation, morning ceremonies, vegetarian breakfast. Advance booking required on the temple website.
Evening walk on the Naksan ramparts—the illuminated wall walk offers views over the rooftops of old neighborhoods. Less than 30 minutes from Seochon.
Late-night shopping in Dongdaemun—the fashion markets open at 10 PM and close at dawn. Wholesalers and independent Korean designers side by side.
Final moment at Han River Park—lay out a mat on the banks, order ramyeon through a Korean app (riverside delivery is a local tradition), and watch the city across the water.
Evening in Gangnam—Apgujeong Rodeo Street for Korean luxury boutiques, or Sinchon for a more student-friendly vibe.
Last-minute shopping in Myeongdong—K-beauty cosmetics, street food (tteokbokki, hotteok), fashion boutiques. The neighborhood is pedestrian in the evening.
Final café in a hanok café in Bukchon or Seochon before heading to the airport.
